revision.text: #REDIRECT [[神体]]

revision.comment: 神体のリダイレクトだが、ご神体ではない場合もあるので新たに起こす。
revision.text:
'''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、一般的に、[[神社]]、[[神宮]]などの境内にあって神聖視、伐採をしないとされる木を指す。この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地であて民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。 [[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。 この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。 山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[依代]] *[[神社]]
{{stub}}

'''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、一般的に、[[神社]]、[[神宮]]などの境内にあって神聖視、伐採をしないとされる木を指す。この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地であて民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。 [[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。 この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。 山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[依代]] *[[神社]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]]

'''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、[[古神道]]における[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）としての[[木]]や[[森]]をさし、[[神体]]のこと。また[[依り代]]・[[神域]]・[[結界]]の意味も同時に内包する木々。
一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、伐採をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地であて民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。 == 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、[[形式]]や[[様式]]が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[依代]] *[[神社]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]]

==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]]

[[画像:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_cedar.jpg|thumb|230px|[[注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:[[由岐神社]]]] '''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、[[古神道]]における[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）としての[[木]]や[[森]]をさし、[[神体]]のこと。また[[依り代]]・[[神域]]・[[結界]]の意味も同時に内包する木々。
一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、伐採をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地にあって民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。 == 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、[[形式]]や[[様式]]が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]

この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]
[[zh:神木]]

{{otheruses}} [[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_cedar.jpg|thumb|230px|[[注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:[[由岐神社]]]] [[File:Big Tree of Shinto Shrine in Kawazu Town A.jpg|thumb|230px|[[杉桙別命神社]]の御神木、[[天然記念物]]]] '''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、[[古神道]]における[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）としての[[木]]や[[森林|森]]をさし、[[神体]]のこと。また[[依り代]]・[[神域]]・[[結界]]の意味も同時に内包する木々。
一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、[[伐採]]をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地にあって民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。
== 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、[[形式]]や[[様式]]が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。

この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。

== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。

{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]
[[zh:神木]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses}} [[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_cedar.jpg|thumb|230px|[[注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:[[由岐神社]]]] [[File:Big Tree of Shinto Shrine in Kawazu Town A.jpg|thumb|230px|[[杉桙別命神社]]の御神木、[[天然記念物]]]] '''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、[[古神道]]における[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）としての[[木]]や[[森林|森]]をさし、[[神体]]のこと。また[[依り代]]・[[神域]]・[[結界]]の意味も同時に内包する木々。'''御神木'''とも称される。
一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、[[伐採]]をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地にあって民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。
== 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、[[形式]]や[[様式]]が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]] *[[春日神木]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]

revision.comment: "Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_cedar.jpg" を "Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg" に差し替え（[[commons:User:MPF|MPF]]による。理由：[[commons:COM:FR|File renamed]]: identity）
revision.text:

== 概要 == 神籬・<ruby>磐座信仰<rp>（</rp><rt>いわくらしんこう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、形式や様式が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、<ruby>社<rp>（</rp><rt>やしろ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]] *[[春日神木]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]

神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]] *[[春日神木]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]

{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月24日 (木) 02:58 (UTC)|独自研究=2017年8月24日 (木) 02:58 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} [[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|230px|[[注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:[[由岐神社]]]] [[File:Cinnamomum camphora 20100601 (Kawazu) (A1).jpg|thumb|230px|[[杉桙別命神社]]の御神木、[[天然記念物]]]] '''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、[[古神道]]における[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）としての[[木]]や[[森林|森]]をさし、[[神体]]のこと。また[[依り代]]・[[神域]]・[[結界]]の意味も同時に内包する木々。'''御神木'''とも称される。
一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、[[伐採]]をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地にあって民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。
== 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、形式や様式が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
先端がとがった枝先を持つ常緑樹が、神が降りる依り代（[[玉串]]）として神事に使われることも多い。玉串として最も一般的なものは[[サカキ|サカキ（榊）]]だが、関東以北では植生上サカキが自生しないため、[[ヒサカキ|ヒサカキ（姫榊）]]や[[オガタマノキ|オガタマノキ（招霊木）]]を用いることもある。一般に玉串は神前に供えるために伐採された枝を示し、神木は大地に根を張ったままの状態を言う。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]] *[[春日神木]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]

一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、[[伐採]]をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地にあって民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。
== 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[神]]や[[命]]や[[自然]]に対する感謝や[[畏怖]]・[[畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、形式や様式が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
先端がとがった枝先を持つ常緑樹が、神が降りる依り代（[[玉串]]）として神事に使われることも多い。玉串として最も一般的なものは[[サカキ|サカキ（榊）]]だが、関東以北では植生上サカキが自生しないため、[[ヒサカキ|ヒサカキ（姫榊）]]や[[オガタマノキ|オガタマノキ（招霊木）]]を用いることもある。一般に玉串は神前に供えるために伐採された枝を示し、神木は大地に根を張ったままの状態を言う。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[依り代]]とされ、[[しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[ナギ]]や[[モチノキ]]、[[スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または[[常世]]（とこよ）と[[現世]]（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって[[禍]]や[[厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[儀式]]や[[祭礼]]を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == 神社等に何らかの関係のある人物が寄進した木などを神聖視し神木として扱われている。
== 造営木 == 神社を造営するにあたりその材木となる木を神木扱いする。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[依代]] *[[神社]] *[[春日神木]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんほく}} [[Category:神社]] [[Category:木]]

一般的に[[神社神道]]の[[神社]]、[[神宮]]の[[境内]]にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む[[鎮守の森]]や、[[伐採]]をしないとされる木を指す。
この他、神社の所有地、民間の所有地にあって民間伝承などの特別な謂われのある木を指す。神社の造営に当たってその木材となる植林または自生する特別に伐採された木を指す場合もある。

神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の[[神殿]]に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[碑]]や[[塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
先端がとがった枝先を持つ常緑樹が、神が降りる依り代（[[玉串]]）として神事に使われることも多い。玉串として最も一般的なものは[[サカキ|サカキ（榊）]]だが、関東以北では植生上サカキが自生しないため、[[ヒサカキ|ヒサカキ（姫榊）]]や[[オガタマノキ|オガタマノキ（招霊木）]]を用いることもある。一般に玉串は神前に供えるために伐採された枝を示し、神木は大地に根を張ったままの状態を言う。

この他、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[神宮スギ]]など景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。

revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/81781230|神木]]"
revision.text:
[[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:由岐神社]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|杉桙別命神社の御神木、[[Natural monument|天然記念物]]]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|大塔宮護良親王桂之古跡（山梨県富士吉田市）]] '''神木'''（しんぼく）とは、[[Ko-Shintō|古神道]]における[[Himorogi|神籬]]（ひもろぎ）としての[[Tree|木]]や[[Forest|森]]をさし、[[Shintai|神体]]のこと。また[[Yorishiro|依り代]]・神域・[[Bai sema|結界]]の意味も同時に内包する木々。'''御神木'''とも称される。
一般的に神社神道の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]、[[List of Jingū|神宮]]の境内にある神体としての木や神聖視される木、その周りを囲む鎮守の森や、[[Logging|伐採]]をしないとされる木を指す。
It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== 概要 == 神籬・磐座信仰（いわくらしんこう）という[[Nature worship|自然崇拝]]も古神道の一部であり、[[God|神]]や命や[[Nature|自然]]に対する感謝や[[Fear|畏怖]]・[[Respect|畏敬]]から、環境が変わる場所にある象徴的なものを、木に限らず神体とした。この古神道から数千年を経る中で、形式や様式が器としての神社や内面としての祭礼が、外来の[[Religion|宗教]]の影響または独自に確立され、神社神道などになっていった。
神の居る場所は、社（やしろ）といわれる神道の神殿に移っていったが、日本に数万ある神社は、もともとは、この古神道における神籬のある場所に建立されたものがほとんどであり、そのことから境内に神籬としての神木や[[Iwakura rock sects|磐座]]としての霊石（岩）やあるいは[[Stele|碑]]や[[Mound|塚]]が存在し祀られている。また古神道と神社神道は、ある部分では共存し不可分でもあるため、神社によっては社を持たず、神木をそのまま神体として祀っているところもあり、また、神社はなくとも自然そのままにある神木が多くの信仰を集めている場所もある。
先端がとがった枝先を持つ常緑樹が、神が降りる依り代（[[Tamagushi|玉串]]）として神事に使われることも多い。玉串として最も一般的なものは[[Cleyera japonica|サカキ（榊）]]だが、関東以北では植生上サカキが自生しないため、[[Eurya japonica|ヒサカキ（姫榊）]]や[[Magnolia compressa|オガタマノキ（招霊木）]]を用いることもある。一般に玉串は神前に供えるために伐採された枝を示し、神木は大地に根を張ったままの状態を言う。
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[Yorishiro|依り代]]とされ、[[Shimenawa|しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[Nageia nagi|ナギ]]や[[Ilex integra|モチノキ]]、[[Cryptomeria|スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]の神宮スギなど景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[Cleyera japonica|サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[Gohei|御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または常世（とこよ）と現世（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって禍や[[Yakudoshi|厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[Bai sema|結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[Ritual|儀式]]や祭礼を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[Dazaifu Tenmangū|太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== 造営木 == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== 関連項目 ==
* [[Shintai|神体]] * [[Yorishiro|依代]] * [[Shinto shrine|神社]] * 春日神木 [[Category:Trees]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:由岐神社]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|杉桙別命神社の御神木、[[Natural monument|天然記念物]]]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|大塔宮護良親王桂之古跡（山梨県富士吉田市）]] The term '''Shinboku''' refers to [[Tree|Tree]]s and [[Forest|Forest]]s as [[Himorogi|Himorogi]] in [[Ko-Shintō|Old Shinto]], as well as [[Shintai|Shintai]]. A tree is a [[tree]], a [[tree]], a [[forest]], a [[Shintai|Shintai]], a [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]], a [[Shinto shrine]], a [[Bai sema|Warding]]. It is also called '''Goshingi'''.
The term ''''Goshingi'''' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū]]s of Shintoism, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not [[Logging]]. It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.

== 関連項目 ==
* [[Shintai|神体]] * [[Yorishiro|依代]] * [[Shinto shrine|神社]] * 春日神木 [[Category:Trees]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:由岐神社]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|杉桙別命神社の御神木、[[Natural monument|天然記念物]]]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|大塔宮護良親王桂之古跡（山梨県富士吉田市）]] The term '''Shinboku''' refers to [[Tree|Tree]]s and [[Forest|Forest]]s as [[Himorogi|Himorogi]] in [[Ko-Shintō|Old Shinto]], as well as [[Shintai|Shintai]]. A tree is a [[tree]], a [[tree]], a [[forest]], a [[Shintai|Shintai]], a [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]], a [[Shinto shrine]], a [[Bai sema|Warding]]. It is also called '''Goshingi'''.
The term ''''Goshingi'''' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū]]s of Shintoism, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not [[Logging]]. It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== Overview == The [[Nature worship|Nature worship]] of [[HIMOKURASHINKO]] is also part of the ancient Shinto religion, and out of gratitude, [[Fear|Awe]], and [[Respect|Awe]] for [[God|God]], life, and [[Nature|Nature]], symbolic objects in places where the environment changes were used as deities, not just trees. symbolic objects in places where the environment changes, including but not limited to trees, as Shinto bodies. In the thousands of years that have passed since this ancient Shinto, the form and style of shrines as vessels and rituals as inner life have been influenced by foreign [[religion]]s or established independently, becoming Shinto shrines and other forms of Shinto.
Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as [[Iwakura rock sects (rocks) as sacred trees, [[Iwakura rock sects|磐座]], [[Stele|碑]]s and [[Mound|塚]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as [[Tamagushi|Tamagushi]], a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatamano-ki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatama-noki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. In general, a tadakushi indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== 依代 == '''神依木'''（かみよりぎ）、'''勧進木'''（かんじんぼく）等は神の[[Yorishiro|依り代]]とされ、[[Shimenawa|しめ縄]]などで特別扱いされている。社殿の無い神域などでは御神体として扱われている。[[Nageia nagi|ナギ]]や[[Ilex integra|モチノキ]]、[[Cryptomeria|スギ]]などが多い。
この他、[[Ise Grand Shrine|伊勢神宮]]の神宮スギなど景観維持や、荘厳さを醸し出すために依り代とは別に特別視された木などを神木扱いにしている場合もある。山などで仕事をする物にとって、山の神の依り代として目立つ木を一時的に神木とし、祀る場合もある。
神社神道における儀式で使われ、[[Cleyera japonica|サカキ]]やナギによって作られる[[Gohei|御幣]]も神籬というが、もともとは、古神道における自然にある神木の代用としての、簡易の依り代である。
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または常世（とこよ）と現世（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって禍や[[Yakudoshi|厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[Bai sema|結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[Ritual|儀式]]や祭礼を行うところもある。
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[Dazaifu Tenmangū|太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== 造営木 == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== 関連項目 ==

In the thousands of years that have passed since this ancient Shinto, the form and style of shrines as vessels and rituals as inner life have been influenced by foreign [[religion]]s or established independently, becoming Shinto shrines and other forms of Shinto.
Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as [[Iwakura rock sects (rocks) as sacred trees, [[Iwakura rock sects]], [[Stele]]s and [[Mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as [[Tamagushi|Tamagushi]], a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatamano-ki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatama-noki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. In general, a tadakushi indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''Kami-yorigi''', '''kanjinboku''', etc. are considered to be [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] of the gods, and are given special treatment in [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]. The [[Nageia nagi]] is a [[Yorishiro]] of the gods, and is given special treatment in [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]. Many of them are [[Nageia nagi|nagi]], [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The [[Gohei]], which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]] and Nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== 神域・結界 == 古神道において神籬は、神の宿る場所としての神域、または常世（とこよ）と現世（うつしよ）の端境と考えれ、恐れ敬った。そして人や現世にあるものや、常世に存在する神やまたは、現世にとって禍や[[Yakudoshi|厄災]]を招くものが、簡単に行き来できないように、[[Bai sema|結界]]として注連縄をはり、禁足地とした。現在でも沖ノ島など社や神木や鎮守の森だけでなく島全体が禁足地になっているところも多くある。招福したい時を一定の期間だけ設けて、神木などの結界を解き神を招くという[[Ritual|儀式]]や祭礼を行うところもある。

Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as [[Tamagushi|Tamagushi]], a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatamano-ki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatama-noki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. In general, a tadakushi indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''Kami-yorigi''', '''kanjinboku''', etc. are considered to be [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] of the gods, and are given special treatment in [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]. The [[Nageia nagi]] is a [[Yorishiro]] of the gods, and is given special treatment in [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]. Many of them are [[Nageia nagi|nagi]], [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The [[Gohei]], which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]] and Nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine Realms and Boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, shimenawa were hung as [[bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as Okinoshima Island where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, [[Ritual|Ritual]]s and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== 伝承 == 昔話など、特別な謂われのある木を指す。この他、有名な歌人が歌に詠んだ木などが神木として扱われている。[[Dazaifu Tenmangū|太宰府天満宮]]の梅など歌人が歌にした木などが多い。
== 記念樹 == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== 造営木 == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== 関連項目 ==

[[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]の巻かれた御神木:由岐神社]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|杉桙別命神社の御神木、[[Natural monument|天然記念物]]]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|大塔宮護良親王桂之古跡（山梨県富士吉田市）]] The term '''Shinboku''' refers to [[Tree|Tree]]s and [[Forest|Forest]]s as [[Himorogi|Himorogi]] in [[Ko-Shintō|Old Shinto]], as well as [[Shintai|Shintai]]. A tree is a [[tree]], a [[tree]], a [[forest]], a [[Shintai|Shintai]], a [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]], a [[Shinto shrine]], a [[Bai sema|Warding]]. It is also called '''Goshingi'''.
The term ''''Goshingi'''' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū]]s of Shintoism, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not [[Logging]]. It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== Overview == The [[Nature worship|Nature worship]] called Himikura Shinto is also a part of the ancient Shinto religion. Out of gratitude, [[Fear]], and [[Respect]] for [[God]], life, and [[Nature|Nature]], symbolic objects in places where the environment changes were used as Shinto bodies, not limited to trees. symbolic objects in places where the environment changes, including but not limited to trees, as Shinto bodies. In the thousands of years that have passed since this ancient Shinto, the form and style of shrines as vessels and rituals as inner life have been influenced by foreign [[religion]]s or established independently, becoming Shinto shrines and other forms of Shinto.
Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as [[Iwakura rock sects (rocks) as sacred trees, [[Iwakura rock sects]], [[Stele]]s and [[Mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as [[Tamagushi|Tamagushi]], a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatamano-ki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatama-noki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. In general, a tadakushi indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.

For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.

== Divine Realms and Boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, shimenawa were hung as [[bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. In some places, [[Ritual|Ritual]]s and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== 記念樹 == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== 造営木 == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.

Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as [[Iwakura rock sects (rocks) as sacred trees, [[Iwakura rock sects]], [[Stele]]s and [[Mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as [[Tamagushi|Tamagushi]], a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatamano-ki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. [[Magnolia compressa|Ogatama-noki (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. In general, a tadakushi indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''Kami-yorigi''', '''kanjinboku''', etc. are considered to be [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] of the gods, and are given special treatment in [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]. The [[Nageia nagi]] is a [[Yorishiro]] of the gods, and is given special treatment in [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]. Many of them are [[Nageia nagi|nagi]], [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The [[Gohei]], which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]] and Nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine Realms and Boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, shimenawa were hung as [[bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as Okinoshima Island where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, [[Ritual|Ritual]]s and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.

Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as sacred trees, [[iwakura rock sects]], [[stele]]s and [[mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as ''[[tamagushi]]'', a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'' (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. ''Ogatama no ki'' are sometimes used. In general, a ''tadakushi'' indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Trees]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]]

[[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]s wrapped around the sacred tree:Yuki Shrine]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|The sacred tree of Sugiwabemikoto Shrine, [[Natural monument|Natural monument]].]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|Ohtamiya Gora Prince Katsura's Ruins (Fujiyoshida City, Yamanashi)]] The term '''''shinboku''''' refers to [[tree]]s and [[forest]]s as ''[[himorogi]]'' in [[Ko-Shintō|Old Shinto]], as well as ''[[shintai]]''. A tree is a tree, a forest, a ''shintai'', a ''[[yorishiro]]'', a [[Shinto shrine]], a [[Bai sema|warding]]. It is also called '''''goshingi'''''.
The term ''goshingi'' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū]]s of Shintoism, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not [[logging]]. It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== Overview == The [[nature worship]] called Himikura Shinto is also a part of the ancient Shinto religion. Out of gratitude, [[fear]], and [[respect]] for [[God]], life, and [[nature]], symbolic objects in places where the environment changes were used as Shinto bodies, not limited to trees. symbolic objects in places where the environment changes, including but not limited to trees, as Shinto bodies. In the thousands of years that have passed since this ancient Shinto, the form and style of shrines as vessels and rituals as inner life have been influenced by foreign [[religion]]s or established independently, becoming Shinto shrines and other forms of Shinto.
Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as sacred trees, [[iwakura rock sects]], [[stele]]s and [[mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as ''[[tamagushi]]'', a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'' (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. ''Ogatama no ki'' are sometimes used. In general, a ''tadakushi'' indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.

For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]]

{{Multiple issues| {{More citations needed|date=May 2022}} {{Sources exist|date=May 2022}} }} [[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]s wrapped around the sacred tree:Yuki Shrine]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|The sacred tree of Sugiwabemikoto Shrine, [[Natural monument|Natural monument]].]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|Ohtamiya Gora Prince Katsura's Ruins (Fujiyoshida City, Yamanashi)]] The term '''''shinboku''''' refers to [[tree]]s and [[forest]]s as ''[[himorogi]]'' in [[Ko-Shintō|Old Shinto]], as well as ''[[shintai]]''. A tree is a tree, a forest, a ''shintai'', a ''[[yorishiro]]'', a [[Shinto shrine]], a [[Bai sema|warding]]. It is also called '''''goshingi'''''.
The term ''goshingi'' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū|jingūs]] of Shintoism, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not grown for [[logging]].<ref name="Mertz">{{cite book |last1=Mertz |first1=Mechtild |title=Wood and Traditional Woodworking in Japan |date=2016 |publisher=Kaiseisha Press |isbn=9784860993238 |pages=85,120 |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/Wood_and_Traditional_Woodworking_in_Japa/F3PjDQAAQBAJ}}</ref> It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== Overview == The [[nature worship]] called Himikura Shinto is also a part of the ancient Shinto religion. Out of gratitude, [[fear]], and [[respect]] for [[God]], life, and [[nature]], symbolic objects in places where the environment changes were used as Shinto bodies, not limited to trees. symbolic objects in places where the environment changes, including but not limited to trees, as Shinto bodies. In the thousands of years that have passed since this ancient Shinto, the form and style of shrines as vessels and rituals as inner life have been influenced by foreign [[religion]]s or established independently, becoming Shinto shrines and other forms of Shinto.
Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as sacred trees, [[iwakura rock sects]], [[stele]]s and [[mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as ''[[tamagushi]]'', a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'' (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. ''Ogatama no ki'' are sometimes used. In general, a ''tadakushi'' indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]]

The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'' (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. == Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]] * [[Chinju no Mori]]

{{Short description|Sacred trees in Shinto beliefs}} {{Multiple issues| {{More citations needed|date=May 2022}} {{Sources exist|date=May 2022}} }} [[File:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|thumb|345x345px|[[Shimenawa]]s wrapped around the sacred tree:Yuki Shrine]] [[File:Cinnamomum_camphora_20100601_(Kawazu)_(A1).jpg|thumb|250x250px|The sacred tree of Sugiwabemikoto Shrine, [[Natural monument]].]] [[File:Ootounomiya-katura1.jpg|thumb|Ohtamiya Gora Prince Katsura's Ruins (Fujiyoshida City, Yamanashi)]] The term {{Nihongo|'''shinboku'''|神木}} refers to [[tree]]s and [[forest]]s as ''[[himorogi]]'' in [[Ko-Shintō|Old Shinto]], as well as ''[[shintai]]''. A tree is a tree, a forest, a ''shintai'', a ''[[yorishiro]]'', a [[Shinto shrine]], a [[Bai sema|warding]]. It is also called '''''goshingi'''''.
The term ''goshingi'' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū|jingūs]] of Shinto, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not grown for [[logging]].<ref name="Mertz">{{cite book |last1=Mertz |first1=Mechtild |title=Wood and Traditional Woodworking in Japan |date=2016 |publisher=Kaiseisha Press |isbn=9784860993238 |pages=85,120 |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/Wood_and_Traditional_Woodworking_in_Japa/F3PjDQAAQBAJ}}</ref> It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== Overview == The [[nature worship]] called Himikura Shinto is also a part of the ancient Shinto religion. Out of gratitude, [[fear]], and [[respect]] for [[God]], life, and [[nature]], symbolic objects in places where the environment changes were used as Shinto bodies, not limited to trees. symbolic objects in places where the environment changes, including but not limited to trees, as Shinto bodies. In the thousands of years that have passed since this ancient Shinto, the form and style of shrines as vessels and rituals as inner life have been influenced by foreign [[religion]]s or established independently, becoming Shinto shrines and other forms of Shinto.
Most of the tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan were originally built on the site of Shinto shrines in the ancient Shinto religion, and this is why there are sacred trees as Shinto shrines, sacred stones (rocks) as sacred trees, [[iwakura rock sects]], [[stele]]s and [[mound]]s exist and are enshrined in the precincts. Some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees as their sacred bodies, and some shrines do not have shrines at all, but enshrine sacred trees in their natural state.
In some places, there is no shrine, but the sacred tree in its natural state is worshiped by many people. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used in Shinto rituals as ''[[tamagushi]]'', a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of skewer is [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], but since sakaki does not grow naturally in the north of the Kanto region, [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'' (sacred tree)]] are sometimes used. ''Ogatama no ki'' are sometimes used. In general, a ''tadakushi'' indicates a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.

This form of worship is based on gratitude, fear, and respect for [[kami]], life, and nature. Ancient Shinto practices involved using symbolic objects, such as trees, in places where the environment changed as vessels for Shinto bodies.
Over the course of thousands of years, the form and style of Shinto shrines and rituals have been influenced by foreign religions or established independently, leading to the development of various forms of Shinto. Today, there are tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan, many of which were built on the site of shrines in ancient Shinto religion. These shrines may contain sacred trees, [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]], rock formations, [[steles]], or [[mounds]], and serve as a testament to the history of nature worship in Japan.
In some cases, shrines may not have a physical structure, but rather worship a sacred tree in its natural state. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used as [[tamagushi]] in Shinto rituals, serving as a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of tamagushi is the [[sakaki]] tree, but other species such as [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'']] may be used in regions where sakaki does not grow naturally (areas north of the [[Kanto]] region).
In general, a tadakushi is a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth and worshiped by many people.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the [[everlasting world]] and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.

The term ''goshingi'' refers to trees that are considered sacred or divine in the precincts of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[List of Jingū|jingūs]] of Shinto, as well as the forests that surround them and trees that are not grown for [[logging]].<ref name="Mertz">{{cite book |last1=Mertz |first1=Mechtild |title=Wood and Traditional Woodworking in Japan |date=2016 |publisher=Kaiseisha Press |isbn=9784860993238 |pages=85,120 |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/Wood_and_Traditional_Woodworking_in_Japa/F3PjDQAAQBAJ}}</ref> It also refers to trees that are owned by shrines or by private individuals and have a special origin in folklore. It may also refer to trees that have been specially cut down for planting or growing wild to be used as timber for the construction of shrines.
== Overview == [[Nature worship]], also known as Himikura Shinto, is a part of the [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shinto]] religion that originated in [[Japan]]. This form of worship is based on gratitude, fear, and respect for [[kami]], life, and nature. Ancient Shinto practices involved using symbolic objects, such as trees, in places where the environment changed as vessels for Shinto bodies.
Over the course of thousands of years, the form and style of Shinto shrines and rituals have been influenced by foreign religions or established independently, leading to the development of various forms of Shinto. Today, there are tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan, many of which were built on the site of shrines in ancient Shinto religion. These shrines may contain sacred trees, [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]], rock formations, [[steles]], or [[Mound|mounds]], and serve as a testament to the history of nature worship in Japan.
In some cases, shrines may not have a physical structure, but rather worship a sacred tree in its natural state. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used as [[tamagushi]] in Shinto rituals, serving as a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of tamagushi is the [[sakaki]] tree, but other species such as [[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]] and [[Magnolia compressa|''ogatama no ki'']] may be used in regions where sakaki does not grow naturally (areas north of the [[Kantō region|Kanto]] region).
In general, a tadakushi is a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth and worshiped by many people.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]] * [[Chinju no Mori]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Trees in religion]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Shinto shrine}}

== Overview == [[Nature worship]], also known as Himikura Shinto, is a part of the [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shinto]] religion that originated in [[Japan]]. This form of worship is based on gratitude, fear, and respect for {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, life, and nature. Ancient Shinto practices involved using symbolic objects, such as trees, in places where the environment changed as vessels for Shinto bodies.
Over the course of thousands of years, the form and style of Shinto shrines and rituals have been influenced by foreign religions or established independently, leading to the development of various forms of Shinto. Today, there are tens of thousands of Shinto shrines in Japan, many of which were built on the site of shrines in ancient Shinto religion. These shrines may contain sacred trees, {{transliteration|ja|[[Iwakura rock|iwakura]]}}, rock formations, [[steles]], or [[Mound|mounds]], and serve as a testament to the history of nature worship in Japan.
In some cases, shrines may not have a physical structure, but rather worship a sacred tree in its natural state. Evergreen trees with pointed branches are often used as {{transliteration|ja|[[tamagushi]]}} in Shinto rituals, serving as a substitute for the gods to descend from. The most common type of {{transliteration|ja|tamagushi}} is the {{transliteration|ja|[[sakaki]]}} tree, but other species such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Eurya japonica|hisakaki]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[Magnolia compressa|ogatama no ki]]}} may be used in regions where {{transliteration|ja|sakaki}} does not grow naturally (areas north of the [[Kantō region|Kanto]] region).
In general, a {{transliteration|ja|tadakushi}} is a branch that has been cut down to be offered to the gods, while a sacred tree is one that is still rooted in the earth and worshiped by many people.
== Yorishiro == '''''Kami-yorigi''''', '''''kanjinboku''''', etc. are considered to be ''[[yorishiro]]'' of the gods, and are given special treatment in ''[[shimenawa]]''. The ''[[Nageia nagi]]'' is a ''yorishiro'' of the gods, and is given special treatment in ''shimenawa''. Many of them are ''Nageia nagi'', [[Ilex integra|mochinoki]], and [[Cryptomeria|cedar]].
In addition, some trees are treated as sacred trees, such as the cedar trees at [[Ise Grand Shrine]], which are regarded as special trees separate from the gods in order to maintain the scenery or create a solemn atmosphere. For those who work in the mountains, a tree that stands out as a substitute for the god of the mountain may be temporarily treated as a sacred tree and enshrined.
The ''[[gohei]]'', which is used in Shinto rituals and is made from [[Cleyera japonica|sakaki]] and nagi, is also called a sacred tree, but it was originally a simple substitute for a naturally occurring sacred tree in ancient Shinto.
== Divine realms and boundaries == In ancient Shinto, the Shinto shrine was considered to be a Shinto shrine as a place where the gods dwelled, or a boundary between the everlasting world and the present world, and was feared and respected. In order to prevent people, things in this world, gods in this world, and things that bring misfortune and [[Yakudoshi|evil]] to this world from easily coming and going, ''shimenawa'' were hung as [[Bai sema|walls]], making it a forbidden place. Even today, there are many places such as [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima Island]] where not only the shrine, the sacred tree, or the forest of the local guardian, but the entire island is forbidden. In some places, rituals and festivals are held for a certain period of time when people want to bring good fortune and invite the gods in.
== Tradition == Refers to a tree with a special origin, such as in a folk tale. In addition, trees that were written about in poems by famous poets are treated as sacred trees. There are many trees such as the plum trees at [[Dazaifu Tenmangū]] that were written about by poets.
== Memorial tree == Trees donated by people who are related to the shrine are considered sacred and are treated as sacred trees.
== Construction tree == When building a shrine, the trees that will be used for its lumber are treated as sacred trees.
== See also == * [[Shintai]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinto shrine]] * [[Chinju no Mori]]
